-- 2 of 23 --

	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
How to Catch All the Clients
You’ll Ever Want!
“How do I get clients?”
After years of publishing articles and products, this is still the #1 question we receive here at The
Total Package.
I understand. You can have all the copywriting and marketing skills in the world, but if you don’t have
clients and aren’t making money … nothing else matters.
So I hope you’ll find this report to be the most detailed, yet to-the-point guide available for rapidly
getting the clients you need to make the big bucks as a copywriter and marketer.
The Next Step in Your
Evolution as Professional
When we first published our How to Build a Six-Figure Copywriting Business – which detailed how
any copywriter can go from zero to six-figures – it was an instant hit.
But since that time, we’ve been in touch with so many copywriters and marketers … especially
those who specialize and focus on the Internet … we’ve discovered a variety of additional tips and
techniques not fully detailed in that product.
More than that, we also recognize that merely getting clients isn’t necessarily the holy grail of your
success. What you REALLY need to achieve your dreams as a freelance copywriter and marketer
are:
1. Professional, high-paying clients …
By this I mean clients who have legitimate businesses, who value copywriting to the
point where they pay excellent fees to writers.
2. Who can dependably pay you …
The client must also have the necessary sales, cash flow and dependability so you
know that you’ll be paid and compensated fairly for your work.
3. With great potential for growth and additional income …
The client should also have good products with lots of potential for growth – and want to
grow aggressively through the copywriting and marketing that YOU provide.
4. Who are fun and fulfilling to work with!
Last but not least, you will ideally ENJOY working and communicating with this person
or the people in this business. Don’t discount this factor – it will make a huge difference

-- 3 of 23 --

4 	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
in the long-term quality of your work.
It’s really only when you find clients who meet these four criteria – and you do truly fantastic work
for them – will your copywriting and marketing business take off. What’s more … just one, two or
maybe three clients like this is all it takes to make a substantial income – and potentially cross the
million-dollar-a-year mark.
Which brings us to the real purpose of this report. It’s not merely to help you find any old client and
collect checks.
It’s to Help You Locate and Initiate Win-Win Relationships
with the Kinds of Clients Who Can Make You Rich!
So without further ado, let’s get started …
The way we see it (and the way we’ve seen many other highly successful copywriters do it),
THERE ARE THREE MAIN WAYS to create working relationships with high-quality clients who
meet the above criteria.
They are …
#1: Meet Them in Person
#2: Attract Them Online
#3: Master Your Domain and Contact Them Directly
The rest of this report will focus on how to get these clients through these three channels.
The best part about this is, by understanding and utilizing these three strategies you can build a
powerful stream of potential client leads that can not only fill your available work time – and make
you a lot of money …
… But also to create backups and “insurance” clients/opportunities in case anything ever happens
or things fall through.
Strategy #1:
How to Meet the Best
Possible Clients In Person!
The first way to get clients and probably the most fun is by Networking!
Right off the bat, let me say that this is DIFFERENT networking advice than you’ve probably read
elsewhere.
Networking to get copywriting and marketing clients is unique and in some ways counterintuitive
to what you’d expect. But, when done right, can really lead into some wonderful long-term
relationships.
Nearly every successful copywriter and marketer we’ve met has used networking to build their
careers – and you should be no different.
So here are some tips for getting clients …
First, Go to Where Prospective Clients Are At
Obviously, unless you’re around clients with the potential and capacity to hire you – all the advice

-- 4 of 23 --

5	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
in the world won’t matter much. So you’ve got to get clear on the best places to go.
There are two primary places copywriters go to meet prospective clients:
a) Internet marketing events
Because the Internet is primarily a direct response medium – all Internet marketers and business
owners are in dire need of sales copy. And not just sales letters – they need writers to help them
write their websites … their emails … their squeeze pages … to launch new products … and much
more.
Many of the most successful copywriters on the Web got their start by writing very successful
letters for top Internet marketers. It’s one of the quickest and strongest credibility-builders you can
get.
Plus, Internet marketing events are loaded with small business owners who are learning all kinds
of new ways to grow their business … and they’re eager to expand …
… But, usually, they’re missing one thing: COPY! So as a copywriter you’re in a very desirable
position at these marketing events. Almost every person in the room needs what you have to offer.
So one of the very first things for you to do is get on as many Internet marketing lists as possible.
A very smart thing to do at this point is to set up a brand new, separate Gmail account, JUST for
marketing lists that you’re on. This is important for two reasons: 1) It will allow you to archive all
your marketing emails in one place for easy reference and learning … and … 2) Your personal
emails won’t be mixed with the high volume of marketing messages you’ll receive from being on
these lists.
To get on the appropriate email lists, just visit the websites or Google names of people like …
Rich Schefren, The Guru of Gurus – Business Acceleration Program
Andy Jenkins & Brad Fallen, Stompernet – Stomping the Search Engines
Brian Clark – The CopyBlogger
Frank Kern – Mass Control
Mike Filsaime – Multi Niche Marketing Specialist
Eben Pagan – Business Building Specialist
Jeff Walker – Product Launch Formula
Troy White, Total Package Editor and www.smallbusinesscopywriter.com
Alex Mondossian – Teleseminar Secrets
Stephen Pierce – Basic Training Bootcamp
Michel Fortin – The Success Doctor
Jay Abraham – Optimal Marketing Strategies
You should also check out the following annual events. These are great places to make potential
client contacts. I know, because I’ve picked up copywriters at some of these events myself!

-- 5 of 23 --

6 	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
Target Marketing – www.targetmarketingmag.com/docs/calendar.html
Affiliate Summit – www.affiliatesummit.com
The Big Seminar - www.bigseminar.com
The System Seminar - www.thesystemseminar.com
Direct Marketing Association (DMA) – www.the-dma.org
American Marketing Association – www.marketingpower.com
Specialized Information Publishers Assoc (SIPA) – www.newsletters.org
Early to Rise – www.earlytorise.com
American Writers and Artists Institute (AWAI) – www.awaionline.com
Dan Kennedy, Super Conference & Info Marketing Seminar – www.dankennedy.com
… And then join their email lists. You’ll start receiving a lot of emails (which can be annoying)
– but you’ll also always hear about the latest and hottest marketing events being held around the
country.
Granted, not every guru holds an event each year – but by being on a variety of lists, you’ll be the
first to know when they do.
As you hear about seminars being held throughout the year, figure out which ones you want to
attend. Generally, the premium “no pitching” events are a little better for landing clients – because
the attendee is often more serious. Typically, the more a seminar costs … the more serious the
business owners and marketers in attendance … and the more they’ll be willing to spend on hiring
a copywriter.
The only advantage of a cheaper event is that it costs you less and attendance is usually higher
– so there can sometimes be a greater chance of meeting potential clients.
Remember: These events can be expensive! But if you follow the steps in this report, they can
also be quite lucrative. We’ve gotten a variety of stories from writers who’ve paid as much as
$5,000 to go to an event – but then walked out with $40,000 … $50,000 … or even $60,000 in
paid work.
It’s possible, if you network properly.
For example, we had dozens of copywriters pay $5,000 to attend Clayton’s 2006 Power Marketing
Summit – yet many walked away with tens of thousands in added fees and assignments. Kim
Krause-Schwalm stated that she made an extra $138,000 in the 12 months following the event,
entirely because of her attendance.
As an added bonus, attending these types of events will increase your Internet marketing
knowledge and make you more valuable to clients.
So you really get to kill two birds with one stone: You educate yourself, increasing the amount of
value your bring to the table … AND you put yourself around clients who have the capacity to pay
you in the months and years to come.
b) Industry trade shows

-- 6 of 23 --

7	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
Don’t just confine yourself to marketing events! Remember – once you’ve figured out what niche
(or niches) you want to specialize in, it’s a great idea to attend industry specific trade shows and
conferences.
Most people don’t realize it, but almost every type of industry holds big trade shows and
conferences where the attendees can learn more about their niche – and meet fellow business
owners.
One such example is something held in Southern California every single year called Expo
West. It’s a huge health conference that’s filled with business owners from all over the country
– business owners who are trying to get exposure for their natural health products. At the event,
there are food makers … supplement manufacturers … beverage companies … and much more.
Granted, not every business at this kind of event uses direct response marketing – but lots do.
And they’re all in the mode of wanting to meet new people who can help them grow.
So it really is the perfect possible environment to plant the seeds for future work.
NOTE: It’s not possible for us to list every industry or market-specific trade show or conference
– but a simple Google search should tell you a lot. Just search whatever niche market you’re in
– along with words like ‘tradeshow’ … ‘conference’ … ‘event’ … ‘gala’ … ‘summit’ … and anything
else that might relate.
The search results will then give you more information about the date, time, frequency and cost of
the events you wish to attend.
Also remember that at these kinds of shows, you’re often going to have to qualify your prospects
a little more than at Internet marketing events – just to make sure they use direct marketing and
value the power of good copy.
But once you do that, the quality of clients you’ll meet at this kind of event can be excellent.
Next – Once You’re at the Event,
Follow this 7-Step Protocol:
1) Bring lots of business cards.
Whether you’re attending a marketing seminar or a tradeshow, you’ll usually be given a name tag
that you hang around your neck. And there will be a little “opening” right behind your name card
that’s perfect for storing business cards.
So before attending any event, get at least 100-200 business cards made that contain: Your name
… your phone number … your email address … your website (if you have one) … your business
name or slogan (if applicable).
It’s not necessary that they be ultra fancy or glamorous – it’s more important that your cards are
readable and professional.
2) Don’t be shy.
Once you’re at the event, be as friendly as possible. Talk to people next to you in line … at your
lunch table … sitting next to you … everywhere you can think of. It’s sometimes easy to be shy,
but remember – people are LOOKING to meet you! You’ll never be ‘rejected’ because attendees
are eager and excited to connect with like-minded individuals.
Plus, everyone is so energized and enthusiastic – it’s very, very natural and easy to just smile …

-- 7 of 23 --

8 	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
say “Hi” … and shake people’s hands.
From that point, you just introduce yourself and then once the other person does the same, ask
them what they do or what business they’re in – and you’ll be off to the races.
3) Favor quality over quantity.
While it can be good to have an ambitious goal about the number of people you want to meet at
a seminar, that can also be a trap. You see, it’s really all about the quality of the relationships and
connections you make.
In other words, you’re much better off making 3-4 really good contacts that 30 average ones. So
how do you tell the difference?
Well, a good contact is someone who …
- Has potential to hire you or connect you …
- You can learn from …
- You enjoy talking to (or is interesting) …
- Has projects pending that you could help with …
- Runs a legitimate business with the capacity to pay well …
Once you gather enough information to know you’re talking with such a person, it’s often a good
idea to spend more time with him or her. Go to dinner with them or hang out at the bar after the
presentations are over.
Again, all it takes is a few really good contacts to fill your work schedule for the entire year!
4) Be interested.
Once you begin talking to a prospective client, the most important thing is to be genuinely
interested in him or her … what this person has to say … and the business they operate.
This isn’t new information – you can find much more about relating to others in books like How
to Win Friends and Influence People – but there’s something magical about being curious and
interested in another person. It’s magnetic and others love being in the presence of someone who
is genuinely interested in them.
Also, the more interested you are in someone – the more they’ll open up and share with you. This
will give you the information you need to tell whether they’ll make a good possible client – and
how ready they are to hire someone like you.
They also will give you indicators of what they’re looking for, so you can know and communicate
whether you’ll truly be able to meet their needs.
The act of being genuinely curious and interested in someone’s business is actually easier than
you think – once you begin to passionately study marketing. You see, everybody’s situation is
different and it’s always intriguing to examine the problems a business faces – and then think
about how you might solve them with your copywriting and direct response knowledge.
So be intensely interested in those you meet – and have FUN!
5) Be cool.
Another crucial thing to remember about networking is to not be needy. Don’t act desperate. Be
COOL. This is easier said than done – especially if you’re just starting out and are really hungry

-- 8 of 23 --

	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
for the work.
But in a networking context, nothing is more annoying than someone who’s overly needy or
aggressive about getting what they want. It’s a huge turn-off and it comes across as very selfish.
So instead, just relax and be cool. When you meet new people – even if they have the potential to
hire you – just be curious about them and interested in what they do. Treat them as friends. Chat
comfortably about their business … about life … about what you do … in a calm, normal way.
Don’t make it obvious that you’re looking for anything. Act as though all your needs are being met
and work is great.
The trick here is not to chase anyone – or to push anyone away because you’re too busy or cool,
either. Rather, you just want to appear OPEN. You don’t really know whether you’re a good match
with the person you’re talking to … but you’re open and curious about whatever might happen.
This is the best place to be, because you’re not needy or desperate – yet you’re receptive to the
possibility of working with the person you’re talking to.
6) Follow up fast.
Okay, so now let’s assume that you’ve networked like crazy. You exchanged dozens of business
cards and made all sorts of good contacts and connections. Many of whom might be able to send
work your way.
So WHAT NOW?
Easy: Follow up very fast. You see, at seminars everyone is in an excited, enthusiastic state.
They’ve met all kinds of people and are riding a huge emotional high.
But the problem is, within days or even hours of the seminar ending, that feeling wears off. And
they begin forgetting everyone and everything that happened during those few days.
That’s why it’s imperative that you “cement” any connections you make with people very quickly
– via email. I recommend that when you meet someone during the day, you follow up with them
that very same night on email (at worst, a day or two later).
You can say something like this …
*****
Hi [Attendee’s name],
It was really great meeting you today! I always appreciate speaking with entrepreneurs who
are passionate about marketing and who will stop at nothing to grow their business.
Anyway, I hope you enjoy the rest of the event and look forward to speaking with you again
soon.
Sincerely,
[Your name]
*****
This is effective because it’s short, positive and it helps solidify the connection you just made. It’s
even better if you can take something specific from your conversation with that person and then
reference or compliment them on it – as this is both impressive … and it increases the sense of
rapport you have with that person.

-- 9 of 23 --

10 	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
Do this with every single person you meet and you’ll have all kinds of powerful possibilities for
future work arrangements!
7) Set up phone calls.
After you’ve sent that initial email wait a few days (no longer than a week) before following up with
them. The difference is that at this point, you want to set up a phone call with those who’ve got
potential for deals.
Often you will have gotten a reply to your first email – but even if you didn’t, you can follow up with
something like this …
*****
Hey [name],
I’m curious about the process you’re using to build your list and sell your heart supplements
[here you can mention anything you learned about their business].
I’d love to talk more about that someday soon – as I have some ideas you might be able to
use.
Let me know if there’s a day and time that works for you to chat over the phone.
Talk to you soon,
[Your name]
*****
Feel free to modify this email based on your own unique circumstances. The exact words don’t
really matter – the key is to ask about a specific part of their marketing or business that interests
you, and where you may be able to help.
This will help to set up a no pressure conversation where you and the prospective client can
discuss his or her problems and needs – and where you can demonstrate your capacity to
understand and offer solutions.
It’s very natural for such conversations to transition into a discussion about how the two of you
may work together – and then it’s up to you to set up a deal and finalize the agreement. You’ll find
out exactly how to do that in the DVD you received titled Pitching the Deal.
Strategy #2:
How to Attract Desirable Clients Online,
Using a Website or Ads
While contacting clients directly (strategy #1) can be amazingly effective – because it allows you
to select the type of client according to your interests and passions …
… Attracting clients with a website (strategy #2) is fantastic for another reason: It puts you in a
position of power when talking to them.
You see, when you have a solid website in place that effectively builds you up and sells your
services – clients will come to you “pre-sold” … meaning, they’re already well on their way to
being convinced that they want to work with you.
From that position, it’s easier to close the deal and set up an arrangement.
There are two primary things you’ll need to effectively build a copywriting/consulting website that

-- 10 of 23 --

11	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
attracts prospective clients:
1. You usually need to have some credentials that you can point to. Some examples of
credentials are …
- Success stories/winning sales letters: Any instance where your letter has generated
impressive sales or response numbers …
- Documentation of results: You could cite specific sales numbers that your letter or
campaigns have done …
- Expert endorsements: Quotes from any notable figure or expert in the industry who has
been impressed with your work or results …
- Client testimonials: Where past clients sing your praises – the more prestigious the
client, and the more specific the praise – the better this is …
- Quality samples: Examples of your work that represent what the client can expect to
receive from you for his money …
- Demonstrations of expertise: When you don’t have tons of successes or winning
samples to show, that you can at least show off your writing talent and knowledge through
articles, blog posts, podcasts, videos and more …
Some good examples of these elements are on the following websites:
www.carlinecole.com
www.copymillmarketing.com
www.responseink.com
www.deannablanchard.com
www.freshcopynow.com
In addition to all of this, you also want …
2. Ideally, you will have a unique position, focus or angle that sets you apart from the other
potential writers and marketers out there. Some ways to do this …
- Specialize in a particular niche. The process just outlined helps a lot in this regard, and
there are countless ways you can specialize and claim a top position in your niche. You
could be “The #1 Health Supplement Copywriter on the Internet” (if writing copy for Internet
supplements was your focus … or Investment … or Dating … or Golf, Gardening, Product
Launches or whatever area you choose to focus in) …
- Add a unique feature to your service or offerings. For example, if you’re fast – you
could call yourself the “The Fastest Copywriter on the Web” (but only do this if one of your
main points of differentiation was that you had really quick turn-around) … or you could
focus on how you guarantee your results – and you’ll do as many re-writes as it takes for
you to beat your clients existing letter … the list goes on.
The key here is to focus on a unique feature/benefit combination that other copywriters
and marketers are not showcasing – and that is highly desirable to potential clients. In the

-- 11 of 23 --

12 	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
case we just mentioned regarding speed – many clients find themselves in tight situations
where they need copy done fast, and you could strongly appeal to such clients with this
positioning …
- Or perhaps you have access to special or “secret” knowledge. For example, if you’ve
written a lot of copy for top Internet marketers – you might be able to show your client how
– just by virtue of hiring you – he’s accessing tons of insider information that you’ll put into
the work you do for him. And he will profit as a result.
The trick with any of these points of differentiation is again fairly simple: a) do your best to
offer things that other writers or marketers aren’t (or at least, things that your competition is
not showcasing) … and b) make sure it’s something desirable to the client – something that
will help attract the type of client you’re looking for!
With these two pillars in place … credibility/proof that you can point to AND a unique
position or specialization … you’re ready to begin constructing your website.
As you build your site, know that there are several formats you can use. Many copywriters use a
sales letter to sell themselves and their services. This can work very well – especially if you have a
lot of successes or a really interesting story about what made you so good at what you do.
Here, you will follow the typical format that you would with any other sales letter … an eye-
catching headline … benefits to the client … a powerful lead … a story or anecdote … proof and
documentation that verifies how and why you’ll be able to deliver … then a “call to action” so that
the prospective client calls you.
For some effective examples of this, please go to:
www.overnight-copy.com
www.smallbusinesscopywriter.com
www.tinawrites.com
www.pagesmith.com
Here are some examples of Niche Market websites:
www.pkscribe.com – Fundraising
www.aparcher.com – Health & Wellness
www.copybymccrea.com – Financial
www.insurancecopywriting.com
www.rensch.com – B2B
The first website belongs to Harlan Kilstein – a controversial “guru” in the copywriting and
marketing community. We mention this not as an endorsement, but because we know he’s booked
quite a bit in freelance services using that website.

-- 12 of 23 --

13	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
As far as we know, the other websites we listed have done pretty well also. So without stealing the
copy, notice some of the strengths and selling points contained on these sites (many of which are
listed in this report) … and then see what you can use in your own efforts.
One thing that’s very effective to have on your sales letter site is to finish with a form that pre-
qualifies the client and then offers a free 30 minute consultation. You can see an example of this
on our ResponseInk.com website under Consulting, or check out the overnight-copy.com website.
This form would ask the client questions about the following …
- The name and website of his business
- How long he’s been in business
- What his current sales numbers are (optional – he may not want to reveal this)
- What goals he has for the growth of his business
- How many products he has in place
- What the price range of his products are
- How is he getting traffic to his site/what volume of traffic is he getting?
- What marketing strategies/systems is he using
- What’s his conversion sequence? (This is the process of how he turns a visitor or an email
address to a customer)
And then anything else you can think of that will help you do a better job.
Offering a free consultation – in conjunction with this kind of form – accomplishes three things:
1. It gives the prospective client a compelling reason to respond to you. Think about
it: Clients are no different than customers. They’re often very busy and thinking only about
themselves. So when you build a website talking about how great you are and what you’re going
to do for them, it’s still easy for the client to put off or delay contacting you.
But when you offer them a free consultation that provides immediate value when they call you – it
gives them another powerful, selfish reason to respond. It adds benefit and urgency.
2. This consultation allows you to demonstrate your expertise, risk-free! Again -- put
yourself in the client’s shoes: He’s going to get a free consultation where he gets to talk about the
problems he’s experiencing in his business … his goals … and more.
And once he gets on the phone with you to talk about all this, it’s the perfect opportunity for you to
demonstrate that you understand his problems and needs … that you know what it’s going to take
for him to achieve his business goals … and, most importantly, that YOU are the missing link. If
you work together, you can give him the spark he needs to grow and profit like never before.
3. It makes you look very professional and valuable – as though you’re “pre-qualifying” him
as a client. You see, when you have clients contacting you like this through your website – not all
of them will be ideal. So this process of evaluating them through your form and free consultation is
the perfect way to weed out clients you simply do not want to work with.
Plus, it makes you look more selective and professional. You’re not some desperate copywriter
or marketing consultant who will work with anyone. Rather, you’ve got standards and criteria
because your time is valuable. You simply can’t afford to waste time or get involved with a

-- 13 of 23 --

14 	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
company who you won’t be able to explode.
If You Don’t Want to Use a Sales Letter,
You Can Also Offer a More Traditional Website
Selling Your Services
Of course, a sales letter-based website is just one option for attracting clients. You can also use
a more traditional-looking multi-page website that showcases your samples … describes your
services … features testimonials and endorsements … and more.
You can also have a blog where you give away valuable information related to your market or to
copywriting/marketing – as a way to demonstrate your knowledge and drive traffic.
The possibilities are endless.
Here are a couple of examples:
www.michelfortin.com
www.carlinecole.com
www.mikejezek.com
www.directcreative.com
The advantage of a more traditional site like this is that it can sometimes come across as more
professional and less pushy. The prospective client can comfortably browse your site without
feeling like you’re pushing or “hard selling” him. (Remember, when you sell your services too hard
– you risk coming across as needy or desperate – and thus not very desirable in a prospective
client’s eyes.)
The approach you use – whether it’s a sales letter or a more traditional website – will often depend
on your market. If your market is less formal and more accustomed to storytelling and aggressive
marketing (like the Internet marketing community is) – then a sales letter will often work fine.
But if it’s a little more formal and conservative, you’ll probably want to go with a cleaner,
professional-looking site with multiple pages.
Next, You’ll Have to Learn
How to Drive Clients to Your Website
Obviously, it’s not enough to have a nice website in place. You’ve also got to get it viewed by
prospective clients so that you have a shot at actually closing deals!
And even though the whole issue of driving traffic is HUGE and much beyond the scope of this
report – there ARE some simple things you can do to get your website in front of prospective
clients …
1. You can run banner ads on websites likely to be visited by prospective clients. Get
creative here and think for a second … Where are your prospective clients likely to be? On natural
health forums? On investment trading websites? On marketing blogs?

-- 14 of 23 --

15	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
Once you’re clear on this, you go to the owner of the website and set up a deal where he displays
your banner ad for a flat fee … or for a commission based on the sales you make.
The banner ad is simply a small, clickable ad that grabs your prospect’s attention and motivates
him to click on it to find out more – which then redirects to your website selling your services.
If you’ve got multiple banner ads running on sites frequently visited by prospective clients
– chances are high you’ll have plenty of quality leads coming to you. Then it’s just a matter of
running them through your sales process … filling out your form … calling you on the phone for
their free consultation … and then transitioning to an official work arrangement).
2. You can also do the same with Google Adwords and Google sponsored ads. Just like
banner ads, you can use PPC and sponsored ads to grab the attention of potential clients who
are searching for copywriting and marketing services … and then get them to click on the link that
drives them to your site.
With these types of ads, the trick is to find the right key words that are going to be used by your
ideal/desired client – and then have your Google ads show up ONLY when people search for
those terms.
Once you’ve done that, you’ve got to make sure your costs for running the ads don’t get too high.
You’ll want to calculate your conversion rate (the number of clients you find versus the cost it
takes to generate your leads) – and make sure it’s in an acceptable range for you.
For example, you might have to spend $500-1,000 in Google ads each month to obtain two new
assignments. Now let’s say that you charge $6,000 per assignment. It’s up to you to determine
whether spending $2,000 to obtain $12,000 in work is acceptable for you or not.
3. Another option is to post on forums and blogs with links to your website. This is cool
because it’s free. Again, just find blogs and forums where prospective clients are likely to visit
– and then post with your website below your name in your signature.
If your posts are informative and demonstrative of your expertise, there’s a chance that
prospective clients will click on the link below your name … go to your website … and then contact
you about working together.
Position of Power
In this section, we’ve covered the elements you need in place for your website to sell your copywriting
and marketing services … how to construct and position your site so that you stand out … and then
how to get prospective clients to your site.
This is a powerful channel to have in place because – as mentioned earlier – the clients who come
to you this way will often be pre-sold and pre-qualified. So you really get to operate from a position of
power.
Plus, it’s passive – so you can have clients learning about you and responding – while you do
nothing. Sure, you will have to talk to them and eventually do the work … but it’s nice to not have to
go out and chase clients. It’s a good feeling to have them coming to you.
Remember – this process will become more and more effective for you as your track record,
reputation and portfolio of samples improves.
So get your site up and then work hard to produce winners and create high-quality samples for
yourself … because the more you have, the easier it will be for your website to attract the clients you

-- 15 of 23 --

16 	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
desire!
Strategy #3:
Be the Master of Your Domain
and Contact Them Directly
While you’ve probably had the experience of trying to contact a potential client directly and look for
work … we believe that the BEST way to do this is by focusing on a niche market.
Let me explain: To write effective copy – especially in the long run – you’ve got to be passionate
about the material.
The most ideal scenario with any client is always to get paid for work that you’d gladly do for free
– actually, the ideal is to get paid for work that you’re already doing for free!
For example, let’s say that you’re an avid golfer. And you’ve purchased golf information products from
a certain company (we’ll call them golf company #1). In doing so, let’s say that you also did some
research and have really begun to understand the whole “improve your golf game” market.
The really cool thing is that – throughout this process of searching and buying products from golf
company #1 – you’re also going to begin seeing sales copy for all kinds of competitive products
– products being marketed by other companies. You’ll start to see the various offers … the benefits
being promised … the marketing strategies being used … and much more.
Combined with being an already excellent copywriter, this will begin to make you a master of the “golf
market.” You will be able to tell all the little differences between the various approaches being sold …
what their strengths and weaknesses were … the quality of their promotions … and so much more.
This Knowledge Makes You
Irresistible to Potential Clients!
Combined with whatever credentials and samples you have as a copywriter, you’ve just multiplied
your value to a potential client many times over. You see – when you know a market really well and
are passionate about it -- this makes a HUGE difference in your ability to write winning copy. And also
to formulate the most effective strategy.
The next few sections of this report will show you exactly how to achieve this degree of mastery in the
shortest possible time.
Quite often, we’ve received stories from Total Package readers who’ve contacted potential clients
once they’ve achieved this level of market mastery … and then merely ask the client questions about
his products – or in certain instances – to give him or her some quick pointers for improving their
sales copy …
… and almost on the spot, they were offered high-paid copywriting/consulting deals!
In several cases, these were deals that other writers would kill for. Especially writers who were
passionate about the subject.
Here are some questions that will help you select the right niche – and achieve similar results …
• Have you purchased multiple products in the same niche? It could be in the area
of …

-- 16 of 23 --

17	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
Alternative Health
Nutritional Supplements
Health Newsletters
Health & Wellness
Weight Loss
Fitness
Exercise Equipment
Yoga
Beauty
Cooking
Financial Newsletters
Financial Planning
Investment Services
Options Trading
International Currency Trading
Real Estate
Retirement
Politics
Fundraising
Personal Development
Spiritual
Self-Help
Opportunity Seeking Products
Entrepreneur Products
Dating
Internet Marketing
Copywriting
Foreign Language Studies
Eductaional
Child Development
Poker
Gardening
Tavel
And many more
Remember: Virtually everyone on the planet is fascinated with at least one subject – and
certainly you can’t be any different!
• Does the niche where you’ve made your purchases use direct response marketing?
This is a big one. Not all markets rely on direct marketing – many focus more on brand
advertising and are not all that ideal for the skills you’ve been developing.
But the good news is, pretty much any company selling online is using direct response in
some form. After all, they’re attempting to sell products off their website – the copy for those
products must do its job … and you’re the perfect person to make it sell better!
• Is the niche growing or large enough for you to be rewarded? You don’t want to get
caught selling knitting yarn to grandmothers in Mississippi. While that may be fun for some,
it’s NOT the kind of niche that has the potential to really make you money.
So look for growth within a niche. You can often tell this purely by searching for important
key words and then seeing how many paid ads (sponsored ads and Google adwords) are
being run … by the number of new companies coming out … by companies creating more
and more new products … and so on. The more you see this kind of thing happening, it’s
generally okay to assume that it’s healthy and growing.
• If none of this fits you so far, is there a niche where you’ve always wanted to
purchase products? Is there a subject you’d like to study? Maybe for you it’s a foreign
language. Or maybe it’s sexual techniques. Or perhaps it’s how to become a screenwriter.
The possibilities are endless. And the point is to find what you’re most interested in … see what

-- 17 of 23 --

18 	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
products are being sold online … and then begin the buying/learning process.
Once you’ve done that, you’ll increase your market mastery and client-getting power by …
Noticing Key Differences Between
Company A’s Products and
Company B’s Products
Okay, so let’s go back to our example about golf products. Let’s say that you’ve purchased
products from one company and you’ve really become passionate about the market. You think you
may want to really master it – and do a lot of writing and marketing for golf products.
The next thing you’ll want to do is start purchasing products from the most successful companies
and competitors in a single niche. You don’t need to own every product (that would be too
expensive and time consuming). But you should do your best to own the most popular ones.
Doing this will give you a very keen sense of the different approaches being used. For example,
in the golf product market … some companies advocate a “natural” approach to correcting your
swing – just relax, visualize a certain outcome, keep your eye on the ball, etc.
While others might teach a “scientific” approach – very specific pointers, systems and techniques
are being used.
Each camp has reasons why they advocate their particular approach. And most niche markets
work like this. There will be various viewpoints and approaches being recommended – and
your job will NOT be to immediately claim one is better than another. Rather, it will be to
fully understand all the relevant details, the selling points and benefits … the strengths and
weaknesses … and so on.
This is one of the fastest ways to become an expert and master of a market – and it’s will help you
tremendously once you strike a relationship with a client. More on this in a second.
Now let’s ask …
“What Could Be Improved About These Products?”
Here’s where you really start to build value for yourself.
You see, most business owners are often too busy (or too lazy!) to buy their competitors’ products
and review/study them.
But when you buy multiple products and then carefully review differences between them, you
quickly start to see … qualitative differences … different formats being used (downloadable audio,
physical DVD, written/print materials, etc.) … different points of emphasis … and so much more.
The more you do this, the more you’ll be able to evaluate the quality of various products in
your niche. Product A may not be detailed or “step-by-step” enough. Product B might be in a
downloadable format and feel less valuable. Product C might target a benefit or attempt to solve a
problem that the prospect doesn’t even care about.
So take note of what you liked and didn’t like about each product. And think in terms of value
– which one gave you the highest sense of perceived value.
That means you also should look at the various offers. Did one product deliver more at a better

-- 18 of 23 --

19	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
price? Did it offer better bonuses? Did it include better customer service or follow-up?
Jot down all your notes and observations about each product – as you may end up using this
information one day. And even if you don’t specifically use it, you’ve gained an understanding and
overall confidence that’s priceless.
What Are Their Promotions Like?
Once you have various products in your hands, it’s very helpful to match up each piece of copy to
the actual product itself.
Did the company use a long copy landing page to sell the product? On a scale of one to ten,
how good is it?
What promises were made? In other words, what benefits did the company focus on?
What credibility elements did they use? How did they prove that the product would deliver? Did
they use track record? Testimonials?
How did the company bribe you for reading the copy? By telling stories? By giving you
instructional information?
If you’re dealing with information products, examine the bullets. How good are they? Did
they seduce you into reading more and wanting the product? Go back and forth between the
product and the copy and see if you can understand how the writer created the bullets. This will
REALLY help you get to know the product and the copy!
What about the offer? Was it clear? How compelling was it? Did you feel like you were getting an
amazing deal?
Overall, did the product live up the expectations created by the sales copy? Or did you feel
a little ripped off?
Once you have the answers to all these questions, you should again take notes. Keep a running
word document where you make notes about the various products you’ve purchased in your niche
… your thoughts and observations about the copy … and more.
The really cool thing is, if you simply follow what I’ve laid out here – you’ll have insights and
suggestions that 9 out of 10 other writers never see or consider. Also, there’s lots of additional
tips and strategies for market research in our Ultimate Desktop Copy Coach copywriting course
– which will only help your mastery and ability to land clients.
As you come up with answers to these kinds of questions, just make note of them and have
everything ready when you actually talk to the client.
There’s just one more thing you should do to build your market mastery – before contacting any
clients. You must ask …
What’s the Marketing Model and Strategy
Used to Sell the Product?
Remember, copy is only one part of the marketing equation. If you really want to master a market
that you’re passionate about – and attract the best possible clients – then you need to be well-
versed in all the marketing models and strategies being used.
This way, when a client does begin working with you, you can create a strategy that goes beyond

-- 19 of 23 --

20 	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
what anyone else is doing.
So let me define marketing strategy in this context. On the Web, I’m referring to the overriding
system that a company is using to promote their products. We covered this in another one of the
free reports you received, called The 5 Internet Marketing Models I Use to Make Millions.
For example, some companies may offer a free newsletter and then they make pitches for the
product within the newsletter. The newsletter provides excellent actionable content as well as
testimonials and success stories from those who are using the products and succeeding.
If this is the marketing strategy, you should look at how the company is building their list … Do
they have a full-blown squeeze page? Or just an opt-in box on their home page?
And how often are they sending out content? Are they selling/pitching more than they’re giving
away content? How do you feel when you open their emails? Are you excited to be getting cool
new information? Or do you dread getting another bad sales pitch?
Some companies don’t offer an actual newsletter – just a series of autoresponder emails that they
package as an “e-course.” Yet others just blast out small amounts of content mixed with pitching in
every single email.
Also, look at the banner ads and PPC (pay per click) ads these companies are using. What’s
being promised in the banner ads? Where are they being placed? Where do their PPC ads rank
on the page?
Are any of the companies using the product launch formula by Jeff Walker? (If you don’t know
what this is, you definitely need to research it immediately!) Are they upselling you at the point of
sale? Are they cross-marketing other products to you once you buy the first one?
All of these details fall within the area of marketing strategy and they’re essential for you to know.
Again take notes of everything you observe. Keep it in your running document about your niche.
And for the products that you’ve purchased, think about how you might improve the strategy being
used to sell them.
If you can point to examples where a company is falling short in their marketing – and you can
offer a solution based on results you’ve seen other companies experience using the RIGHT
marketing strategy – it quickly positions you as the expert.
The client will almost automatically think and assume that you can help him – purely based on the
fact that you see a distinction and solution that he’s not aware of.
Speaking of clients, you’re just about ready to contact one. So ask yourself …
“Whose Products Do I Like Best?”
And who’s got the greatest potential? At this stage, you want to figure out the company you’d most
want to work with in the niche market you’re mastering.
After all, you’ve tried a variety of products and you’ve thought carefully about their various
strengths and weaknesses. It’s only natural that you will have an opinion about who’s right …
who’s approach works best … and who you can help most.
This is most likely the first company you want to develop a relationship with. The only caveat
is that you want to be SURE this company has potential to grow and pay you accordingly.
Sometimes you might fall in love with a company that’s simply not ready to pay you what your

-- 20 of 23 --

21	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
worth. It’s a bummer, but you have to move on.
For example, as I explained earlier, the golf market has all kinds of approaches and personalities.
Some are more Zen-like and less technical in their approach to improving. Others recommend a
much more systematic, scientific means of practicing and improving. The list goes on.
So if you were going to write in the golf market, you would pick the approach that you resonate
with most and then contact the company teaching that approach.
To do so, just follow these steps …
Develop a viewpoint within the market, and figure out who’s approach resonates
most with you …
Make sure you have a keen understanding of this company’s strengths and
weaknesses within the market, relative to the competition …
Figure out EXACTLY what you’d do if you were in charge of transforming this
company’s sales and profits – beginning with their overall marketing strategy and sales
copy …
Once you’re clear on all this, you’re finally ready to make contact …
Clarify Your Intention for Making Contact
Right before contacting a potential partnering client, it’s critical that you get clear on your intention
for making contact. Here are some potential options for what you want – and what you choose will
depend upon your needs and position at the point of contact.
Here are some possibilities for you …
If you’re too busy, you could just make friends with a company leader – planting the seed for
later interactions or for eventually working with that company (if you’re not ready to take on work
right now) …
You can directly attempt to set up a more in-depth copywriting/consulting relationship (I’ll
break down exactly how to do this in just a moment) …
If you’re relatively new, you can offer to write “on performance” – where you attack one of
their current product/promotions and they pay you only if your copy does better than what they
currently have going …
Whatever you do will depend on your needs, desires, level of experience and schedule.
And once you decide, it’s time to make the call or email …
Contact the Prospective Client to
Discuss His or Her Precise Goals
A client’s goals are the most important thing you must consider when looking for a more in-depth,
win-win relationship.
So here’s the procedure – whether you call or email the company leader …
Introduce yourself as a customer and working professional. Say something like, “Hello
John Doe, My name is [Your Name] and I’m a customer of yours. I love your products! I’m
also a professional copywriter and direct marketing expert. While I think you’re doing a
pretty darn good job in your marketing, I also believe there are some easy things you could
1.
2.
3.
1.

-- 21 of 23 --

22 	How to Catch All the Clients You’ll Ever Want!
do that could considerably improve your results. I’d love to talk to you about them. Is there
a day and time that’s good for you?”
When you make contact, ask him that all-important question, “What are your goals?”
Explain that you’ve purchased a lot of different products in the market and you’ve seen
everyone else’s marketing. You believe that his company has tremendous potential – and
you’re curious about where he wants to go in the next 1 year, 3 years, 5 years, etc.
You really want him to paint the ideal picture for his business and life – partly because that
puts him in a positive emotional state, and because he will then associate those emotions
with you!
Then just sit back and LISTEN! Remember, there’s not a person on this planet who
doesn’t enjoy being listened to. And company owners LOVE nothing more than to talk
positively and optimistically about their goals, direction, aspirations and more. So indulge
them – this way you’ll also have a much better idea of how you can help.
Think about what they’ve said, and then state your intention. If you don’t have time to
take on work from them, then just say something like, “Look, I’m really busy with my current
projects but I just wanted to commend you on everything you’re doing and give you some
simple suggestions for improvement. You’re free to call me or email anytime.” Then you can
give him the suggestions right then and there, or you set up a later call to go deeper.
If you want to set up an immediate work/partnering/consulting relationship – it’s
imperative that you demonstrate value. In this context, demonstrating value is easy with
all the pre-work you’ve done before establishing contact. All you do is speak intelligently
about a client’s products … the strengths and weakness of his products … how his
marketing could be improved … what his competition is doing that he’s NOT doing … then
you become an immediate expert in his eyes. And because of this expertise and value, it
becomes virtually impossible to turn you away. In fact, he may attempt to hire you just to
keep you from approaching his competition!
At this point, he will probably ask you for samples of past work – and will begin discussing a
potential project with you (or asking you where you want to start.)
Don’t worry if you don’t have a TON of great samples at this point, either. That will come with time
– and your samples won’t be quite as necessary at this point (if you’ve followed this process)
because you’ve demonstrated value and expertise in some other great ways.
Which is also why this process is the best way for beginners to get great clients.
If this conversation goes well and the client is looking to hire someone – chances are excellent
that you’ll get the job. And it’s at this point that you begin making the transition to discussing the
deal, your fee/compensation … the work arrangement and more. (Please see other parts of this
product for more on how to set up a powerful deal and contract.)
Turn your Passions into Profits!
Again, I hope you see the beauty of this process. What it does is take an area you’re already
passionate and excited about – and helps you become a master of that market. Once you achieve
that basic mastery, you leverage it in conjunction with your copywriting and marketing skills to make
tons of money doing work you love.
Few writers and marketers actually consider the elements of this process when they’re looking for
2.
1.
2.
3.

-- 22 of 23 --



-- 23 of 23 --
